title: Labour market statistics (income): June 2025 quarter | Stats NZ
content:
## Labour market statistics (income): June 2025 quarter
27 August 2025, 10:45am
27 August 2025 : We have corrected an error in the annual gender pay gap change. The figure is a 3.0 percentage point change, not a 3.0 percent change as originally published in the key facts.
Labour market statistics (income) reports on information about income collected annually from the Household Labour Force Survey (HLFS). It provides information about individual and household income, including wages and salaries, self-employment, and government transfers income, both at an aggregate level and for subpopulations of interest.
In the year to the June 2025 quarter, median weekly income from:
- all sources was unchanged at $959
- wages and salaries increased by $37 (2.8 percent) to $1,380
- government transfers increased by $19 (4.1 percent) to $472.
Median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased by $1.44 (4.3 percent) to $35 in the year to the June 2025 quarter.
The gender pay gap was 5.2 percent in the June 2025 quarter, down 3.0 percentage points from 8.2 percent in the June 2024 quarter.
All income data refers to gross (before tax) income for individuals and is statistically significant unless otherwise stated.
In the year to the June 2025 quarter, median weekly income for disabled people from:
- all sources increased by $20 (4.0 percent) to $518
- government transfers increased by $26 (5.7 percent) to $488.
Median weekly income for disabled people from wages and salaries was $1,163 in the June 2025 quarter, compared with $1,120 in the June 2024 quarter. This was not a statistically significant change.
A new disability table has been added to Aotearoa Data Explorer containing information about earnings from wages and salaries by disability status and age group .

## Income from all sources collected
Median weekly income from all sources collected, for all people, remained at $959 in the June 2025 quarter.
Median weekly income from all sources ($) collected for all people, June quarters, 2000–2025
Median weekly income from all sources collected comprises income from wages and salaries, self-employment, government transfers, Accident Compensation Corporation, and includes people with negative or no income.

### Median weekly earnings
Median weekly earnings from wages and salaries were $1,380 in the June 2025 quarter, an increase of $37 (2.8 percent) from $1,343 in the June 2024 quarter.
- For men, median weekly earnings were $1,500, compared with $1,462 in the June 2024 quarter.
- For women, median weekly earnings were $1,247, an increase of $52 (4.3 percent) from $1,195 in the June 2024 quarter.
Median weekly earnings from wages and salaries ($), by sex, June quarters, 1998–2025

### Median hourly earnings
Median hourly earnings from wages and salaries reached $35.00 in the June 2025 quarter, an increase of $1.44 (4.3 percent) from $33.56 in the June 2024 quarter.
- Full-time median hourly earnings were $36.53, an increase of $1.51 (4.3 percent) from $35.02 in the June 2024 quarter.
- Part-time hourly earnings were $27.50 in the June 2025 quarter, compared with $26.50 in the June 2024 quarter. This was not a statistically significant change.
Annual percentage change in median hourly earnings from wages and salaries, by sex, June quarters, 1999–2025
For women, median hourly earnings from wages and salaries rose $1.68 (5.2 percent) to $33.76 in the year to the June 2025 quarter.
For men, median hourly earnings were $35.62 in the June 2025 quarter (up 1.9 percent annually). The change for men was not statistically significant.
Median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased for the following age groups in the year to the June 2025 quarter:
- 15–19 years – up 30 cents (1.3 percent) to $24.00
- 20–24 years – up 70 cents (2.6 percent) to $27.70
- 45–49 years – up $1.59 (4.1 percent) to $40.00
- 50–54 years – up $2.52 (6.9 percent) to $38.96
- 55–59 years – up $2.04 (5.7 percent) to $38.00
- 60–64 years – up $1.64 (4.7 percent) to $36.38.
Women’s median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased for the following age groups in the year to the June 2025 quarter:
- 15–19 years – up 48 cents (2.0 percent) to $24.00
- 20–24 years – up $1.13 (4.3 percent) to $27.50
- 45–49 years – up $2.14 (5.9 percent) to $38.20
- 50–54 years – up $2.64 (7.5 percent) to $37.64
- 55–59 years – up $1.92 (5.7 percent) to $35.48.
There were no statistically significant changes by age group for men’s median hourly earnings from wages and salaries.
Median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased for the following ethnic groups in the year to the June 2025 quarter:
- European – up $1.31 (3.7 percent) to $36.44
- Māori – up $1.76 (5.8 percent) to $32.00
- Pacific – up $1.27 (4.3 percent) to $31.00
- Asian – up $1.43 (4.6 percent) to $32.60.
Women’s median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased for the following ethnic groups in the year to the June 2025 quarter:
- European – up $1.69 (5.0 percent) to $35.39
- Māori – up $1.63 (5.5 percent) to $31.33
- Pacific – up $1.00 (3.4 percent) to $30.00
- Asian – up $2.00 (6.7 percent) to $32.00.
Men’s median hourly earnings from wages and salaries increased for the following ethnic groups in the year to the June 2025 quarter:
- European – up $1.26 (3.4 percent) to $38.00
- Pacific – up $1.97 (6.6 percent) to $31.97.
Changes to other ethnic groups for men were not statistically significant.

### Increases to women’s median hourly earnings by industry and occupation
In the year to the June 2025 quarter, women’s median hourly earnings from main wage and salary jobs increased across the following industries:
- financial and insurance services – up $4.04 (9.4 percent) to $47.23
- transport, postal, and warehousing – up $4.00 (12.9 percent) to $35.00
- construction – up $3.03 (9.5 percent) to $35.00
- public administration and safety – up $2.03 (5.2 percent) to $41.24
- education and training – up $1.99 (5.6 percent) to $37.66
- arts, recreation, and other services – up $1.70 (5.7 percent) to $31.36
- manufacturing – up $1.52 (5.2 percent) to $30.61
- retail trade and accommodation – up $1.00 (4.0 percent) to $26.00.
Over the same time period, women’s median hourly earnings from main wage and salary jobs increased across the following occupations:
- machinery operators and drivers – up $2.15 (8.0 percent) to $29.00
- clerical and administrative workers – up $1.43 (4.5 percent) to $33.56
- professionals – up $1.35 (3.1 percent) to $44.50.

## Government transfers
Median weekly income from government transfers increased by $19 (4.1 percent) to $472 in the year to the June 2025 quarter.
Government transfers include income from New Zealand Superannuation, Veteran’s Pension, Student Allowance, and transfers from Inland Revenue, and Work and Income.

## Gender pay gap at 5.2 percent
The gender pay gap was at 5.2 percent in the June 2025 quarter, down from 8.2 percent the previous year. This means that, on average, women earned 5.2 percent less for an hour’s work than men.
The gender pay gap is calculated using median hourly wage and salary earnings for men and women. Changes to median pay are more representative of pay changes for most workers than changes to mean (average) pay.
Gender pay gap (%), June quarters, 1998–2025
Data for this release comes from the Household Labour Force Survey (HLFS). As the HLFS is a sample survey, and does not interview every person in New Zealand, a small amount of uncertainty is expected for any estimates produced. This uncertainty is often represented as sample errors, which show the bounds of confidence an estimate lies in. Sampling errors can be used to understand whether a genuine (or statistically significant) change has occurred.
With an estimate of 5.2 percent, the gender pay gap had a sampling error of about 1.9 percentage points in the June 2025 quarter. This means we are largely confident that the gender pay gap lies between 3.3 and 7.1 percent.
Working-class heroes: Understanding labour market data has more information on sampling errors and confidence intervals.
A gender pay gap of 0 percent would imply no difference in earnings between men and women. This measure does not consider factors that could influence differences in earnings, such as occupation, qualifications gained, age, and hours worked.
Measuring the gender pay gap has more information on gender pay gaps, how to calculate them, and why we use median pay.
Organisational gender pay gaps: Measurement and analysis guidelines explains how to calculate the gender pay gap within your own organisation.
Gender pay gap toolkit is a resource provided by the Ministry for Women including a tool to calculate the gender pay gap within your organisation.
Aotearoa Data Explorer – Income provides this data. How to use Aotearoa Data Explorer contains video tutorials and a user guide.

## Definitions and metadata
Labour market statistics (income): June 2025 quarter – DataInfo+ includes period specific notes for the June 2025 quarter.
Labour market statistics – DataInfo+ has general methodology used to produce labour market statistics (income) statistics.
Labour market statistics (income) concepts – DataInfo+ has definitions of terms used in this release.

Labour Market Statistics unit info@stats.govt.nz
Labour market statistics (income): June 2026 quarter will be released in August 2026.
